text: The video shows a patient with significant adhesions and a large previous incision visible on her right upper quadrant, likely from an old cholecystectomy.
The resident effectively dissects around the vena cava, with the left lateral section of the liver visibly mobilized.

The surgeon identifies and points out the large phrenic vein branches entering the left side, with the middle branch visible nearby. The surgical field is inspected from underneath, ensuring that the structures are free and unobstructed.
The surgeon and resident are taking down the posterior attachments of the liver, focusing on the area near the caudate lobe. The liver appears cirrhotic, likely due to Hepatitis C and B.
The surgeon uses the harmonic HDI device, which efficiently dissects and cuts through tissue with a single button operation.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device with a flat blade to dissect and cut through the liver parenchyma. The harmonic is applied to both the top and bottom sides of the liver, allowing for efficient tissue separation over the first two centimeters. The device is opened and gradually closed to facilitate dissection. Following this, an ultrasound is performed before proceeding with staple hepatectomy.

The surgeon meticulously advances the harmonic scalpel through the liver parenchyma, carefully closing the blade while assessing for resistance that may indicate atypical vasculature or biliary structures.
